text.heading: Transformers, other power equipment rot away at PHCN’s Ijede store
text.p: Despite the low level of electricity supply in the country, power equipment worth billions of naira including about 745 transformers with capacities ranging between 200 and 300KVA, and insulators as well as other accessories are rotting away at Power Holding Company of Nigeria’s (PHCN’s) store at Ijede near Egbin Power Station.
The Nation’s investigation reveals that similar equipment are abandoned in various locations of the power utility firm, and have been deteriorating over the years.

It was gathered that the transformers were originally procured for the Power Holding Company of Nigeria (PHCN) over five years ago but owing to alleged non-conformity with due process in the procurement, its usage was halted pending clarifications in the transaction.

A source said: "Most of the equipment were imported years back, during the offshore/onshore contracts for rural electrification; but no sooner had they imported the equipment than the Federal Ministry of Power placed a hurdle on the removal of the materials there. The action of the Federal Ministry of Power was based on the allegation that the contract process was not properly managed. A committee was set up to investigate that allegation and after that, a report was submitted, which was eventually reviewed.
"Simultaneously, there was a presidential directive that all offshore/onshore contracts should be converted to Turnkey, taking into cognizance that materials are available here.
"But because of the closure of the stores, nobody had any power to come and touch the materials here until now ,"

But with the establishment of the Rural Electrification Agency (REA) whose bill was signed into law in March 2005, and which was inaugurated on the 16th of March, 2006, the Federal Government decided to hand over the equipment to the agency in 2007.
Despite claims by the Rural Electrification Agency that it was handling over 2,010 rural electrification projects spread across the country, the equipment are still decaying at the Ijede.
investigation further revealed that some of the rural projects were nearing completion but fear of theft and vandalism compelled them to leave the equipment where it is.

A staff of the organisof ation who does not want his name mentioned, told our reporter that efforts are being made to put the equipment to use. His words: "We have about 745 transformers here. Some of the rural electrification projects are yet to be completed hence the transformers are here. They have capacities of between 200 and 300 KVA. The reason for not taking them to site is to avoid vandalism and stealing."

When the Mr. Sam Gekpe, Managing of Rural Electrification Agency, met with the members of House Committee on Power and Steel during the committee’s probe of spending in the power sector between 1999 and 2007, he said that the agency had not received a letter from the government authorizing it to have access to the equipment. "The place was actually covered with weeds. We cleared the place and even applied chemical treatments to make sure that the growth of weeds was actually retarded. We found some equipment here that was grounded. And of course, we carried out an inventory. We took stock of everything. Later, there was an instruction that we should keep off the stores here by the Federal Ministry of Power." The authorization was given the agency later, it was gathered.

When the Minister of Power, Lanre Babalola visited the store recently, the former Managing Director of PHCN, Bello Sueliman, told our reporter that the management of PHCN was making moves to woo the Rural Electrification Agency to loan it some of the equipment. But with sack of Suleiman as PHCN chief and arrest of Mr. Sam Gekpe, Managing of Rural Electrification Agency alongside the Chairman of the House Committee on Power and Steel Ndudi Elumelu and others over N5.2 billion power contract scam, efforts to put the equipment into use have been stalled.

Rural Electrification Agency is a product Power Sector Reform Act. Gekpe said that the power sector reform, which started in 1999, was a concept to reposition the power sector. The reform was engineered to sustain efficiency and to ensure that there is cost recovery mechanism put in place in such a way as to increase private investors coming into the sector to actually revitalise it.

He said that given the reform and inauguration of the agency, the agency was mandated to coordinate electricity for the rural areas throughout the country, promote effective electricity activities and establish and operate Rural Electrification Fund (REF). Currently, no substantial achievement has been made by the agency in over three years it came into force.
